name: Brazil

visa_free.notes: Max 90 days per 180-day period (Schengen-style rule applies)

Visa-free up to 90 days under Mercosur freedom-of-movement arrangement. Argentina is a full Mercosur member; entry also permitted with national ID card (no passport required) at land borders. Multiple current June 2026 sources (visabeat.com, joinsherpa.com, search synthesis) confirm Argentina remains visa-free. The December 2025 VisaHQ articles suggesting Brazil would add Argentina to the e-visa regime were speculative about future plans, not confirmed ordinances. The TheTraveler.org (Feb 6 2026) article explicitly confirmed Argentina was NOT under e-visa as of that date. A previous run (2026-06-27) incorrectly moved Argentina to e_visa based on those speculative articles; corrected in 2026-06-29 run.

Visa-free up to 30 days under Inter-Ministerial Ordinance 18/2026 (Open Doors 2026, effective 24 February 2026); extendable in-country to a cumulative 90 days per 12-month period. Previously 90 days under April 2025 QGRV.
Entry also permitted with national ID card
Entry also permitted with national ID card

Visa-free up to 30 days under Inter-Ministerial Ordinance 18/2026 (Open Doors 2026, effective 24 February 2026); extendable in-country to a cumulative 90 days per 12-month period. Covers tourism, business, conferences, and family visits; work assignments still require a temporary visa. Applies to ordinary passport holders. Additionally, a separate reciprocal visa exemption MRE/MTur joint press release (May 2026) also confirms 30-day exemption for tourism/business. Pilot to be evaluated Q4 2026.
Entry also permitted with national ID card

Visa-free up to 30 days under Inter-Ministerial Ordinance 18/2026 (Open Doors 2026, effective 24 February 2026); extendable in-country to a cumulative 90 days per 12-month period. Previously 90 days under April 2025 QGRV.
Entry also permitted with national ID card

Visa-free up to 30 days under Inter-Ministerial Ordinance 18/2026 (Open Doors 2026, effective 24 February 2026); extendable in-country to a cumulative 90 days per 12-month period. France was temporarily placed under e-visa requirement from January 2026 then restored to visa-free under Portaria 18/2026. Previously 90 days per 180-day period under April 2025 QGRV.

Visa-free up to 30 days under Inter-Ministerial Ordinance 18/2026 (Open Doors 2026, effective 24 February 2026); extendable in-country to a cumulative 90 days per 12-month period. Previously 90 days per 180-day period under April 2025 QGRV.

Visa-free up to 30 days under Inter-Ministerial Ordinance 18/2026 (Open Doors 2026, effective 24 February 2026); extendable in-country to a cumulative 90 days per 12-month period. Previously 90 days under April 2025 QGRV.

Visa-free up to 30 days under Inter-Ministerial Ordinance 18/2026 (Open Doors 2026, effective 24 February 2026); extendable in-country to a cumulative 90 days per 12-month period. Previously 90 days under April 2025 QGRV.

Visa-free up to 90 days. Mexico is a Mercosur associate state; entry also permitted with national ID card under QGRV April 2025 provision. Multiple current June 2026 sources (joinsherpa.com, visarequirements.info, search synthesis) confirm Mexico remains visa-free for entry to Brazil. Note: Mexico requires e-visa for Brazilians (from Feb 5 2026), but Brazil has NOT reciprocated with an e-visa requirement for Mexicans as of June 2026. A previous run (2026-06-27) incorrectly moved Mexico to e_visa based on speculative December 2025 VisaHQ articles about future plans; corrected in 2026-06-29 run.
Entry also permitted with national ID card
Entry also permitted with national ID card

Visa-free up to 30 days under Inter-Ministerial Ordinance 18/2026 (Open Doors 2026, effective 24 February 2026); extendable in-country to a cumulative 90 days per 12-month period. Previously 90 days under April 2025 QGRV.
VIVIS issued up to 5 years validity, max stay 90 days, max 180 days per year. Brazil does not maintain diplomatic relations.
Entry also permitted with national ID card
Visa-free up to 60 days. Entry also permitted with national ID card. Venezuelan passports have validity extended 5 years from expiration date per QGRV footnote.

e_visa.notes: E-visa (VIVIS electronic) required. Initially imposed April 10, 2025; permanent e-visa scheme confirmed from January 1, 2026 onward. Apply via VFS Global portal (brazil.vfsevisa.com). Fee approximately R$257 / USD 80.90. Multiple-entry visa, validity up to 10 years (some sources cite 5 years for US). Each stay limited to 90 days, renewable once within a 12-month period. Valid for tourism, business, transit, artistic or sporting activities.

E-visa (VIVIS electronic) required. Initially imposed April 10, 2025; permanent e-visa scheme confirmed from January 1, 2026 onward. Apply via VFS Global portal (brazil.vfsevisa.com). Fee approximately R$257 / USD 80.90. Multiple-entry visa, validity up to 5 years for Canadians. Each stay limited to 90 days, renewable once within a 12-month period. Valid for tourism, business, transit, artistic or sporting activities.

E-visa (VIVIS electronic) required. Initially imposed April 10, 2025; permanent e-visa scheme confirmed from January 1, 2026 onward. Apply via VFS Global portal (brazil.vfsevisa.com). Fee approximately R$257 / USD 80.90. Multiple-entry visa, validity up to 5 years for Australians. Each stay limited to 90 days, renewable once within a 12-month period. Valid for tourism, business, transit, artistic or sporting activities.

visa_policy.notes: Primary source is the official QGRV (Quadro Geral de Regime de Vistos) updated April 8, 2025 (Portuguese-language official PDF, cop30.br) and the January 15, 2026 English QGRV (qgrv-simples-ing-15jan26.pdf on gov.br, blocked by CAPTCHA for extraction). Applies to ordinary passport holders traveling for tourism or business. The Schengen-rule (*) means nationals of those countries may not stay more than 90 days in any 180-day period and cannot extend - now formally codified by Brazil-EU bilateral Decree No. 12,864 (signed and in force March 2, 2026). The # symbol means entry is also permitted with national ID card (no passport required). OPEN DOORS 2026: Inter-Ministerial Ordinance 18/2026 (published 4 March, effective 24 February 2026) grants visa-free entry to China, Denmark, France, Hungary, Ireland, Jamaica, Saint Lucia and the Bahamas for up to 30 days (extendable in-country to 90 days/year cumulative). This reduces the max_stay_days for these countries from 90 to 30. E-VISA EXPANSION: From January 1, 2026, Brazil implemented a permanent e-visa regime covering the US, Canada, and Australia (effective April 10, 2025 and permanent from January 1, 2026); the VFS Global portal fee is approximately USD 80.90 for a multiple-entry visa. France was placed on e-visa briefly then restored to visa-free under Portaria 18/2026 effective 24 February 2026. IMPORTANT CORRECTION (2026-06-29): Argentina and Mexico remain VISA-FREE for entry to Brazil as of June 2026 - they were incorrectly classified as e_visa in the 2026-06-27 run based on speculative December 2025 articles about future plans, not confirmed ordinances. Multiple June 2026 sources confirm both countries remain visa-free. Note: Mexico requires an e-visa for Brazilians entering Mexico (from Feb 5 2026), which is the reverse direction. EU-BRAZIL BILATERAL DECREE: Decree No. 12,864 (effective March 2, 2026) formalizes the 90-days-in-any-180-day-period rolling window for EU ordinary-passport holders visiting Brazil, replacing the prior 'three months in a six-month period' formulation. UK and Ireland are NOT covered by this EU-Brazil decree.

visa_types.name: e-Visa (VITEM II - Visitor Visa)
visa_types.notes: Available to nationals of USA, Canada, and Australia under permanent e-visa regime (effective April 10, 2025 for US/Canada/Australia; permanent from January 1, 2026). Multiple-entry, up to 90 days per visit within 10-year validity (5-year for Australia/Canada). Fee approximately BRL 257 / USD 80.90. Applied via VFS Global portal. VITEM II visa code (visitor visa for tourism/business). France was briefly on e-visa then restored to visa-free (Feb 24, 2026). Argentina and Mexico remain visa-free as of June 2026 - not currently in e-visa regime.

visa_types.name: Work Visa (VITEM V - Temporary Employment)
visa_types.purpose: Employment in Brazil with Brazilian employer
visa_types.notes: Employer-sponsored. Brazilian employer must obtain prior work authorization from Ministry of Justice (via MigranteWeb). Valid up to 2 years, renewable. VITEM V code. Holders may apply for permanent residence after meeting residence requirements. After 4 years residence, may naturalize.

visa_types.name: Student Visa (VITEM IV)
visa_types.purpose: Full-time study at Brazilian educational institutions
visa_types.notes: For enrollment in Brazilian public or private educational institution. Valid for course duration. Requires enrollment confirmation letter. VITEM IV code. Applied at Brazilian embassy/consulate. After completing study, must leave or obtain different authorization to remain.

visa_types.name: Digital Nomad Visa (VITEM XIV - Nômade Digital)
visa_types.purpose: Remote work for foreign employers while residing in Brazil
visa_types.notes: For remote workers employed by or providing services to foreign companies/clients outside Brazil. Minimum income: USD 1,500/month or savings of USD 18,000. Requires health insurance (min USD 30,000 coverage), criminal background check (apostilled), employment contract or proof of freelance income from foreign sources. Initial 1-year validity, extendable 1 year (max 2 years). VITEM XIV code. Based on Normative Resolution RN 45/2021. In-country extension via MigranteWeb (BRL 168 fee). Also covers retirees and pensioners.

outbound_official.notes: Official source confirmed: Itamaraty (Brazilian MFA) Portal Consular publishes 'Tabela de Vistos para Cidadãos Brasileiros' (Visa Table for Brazilian Citizens) at http://www.portalconsular.itamaraty.gov.br/tabela-de-vistos-para-cidadaos-brasileiros, which consolidates visa requirements for Brazilian ordinary-passport (PACOM) holders traveling abroad for tourism and business. A reference/landing page also exists on the canonical gov.br MRE domain at https://www.gov.br/mre/pt-br/consulado-montevideu/servicos-consulares/informacoes-e-lins-uteis/tabela-de-vistos-para-brasileiros (last updated 12/06/2026 14h49). The actual table is rendered by a JavaScript single-page application (Plone CMS) behind Cloudflare bot protection; the country-level destination data could not be extracted programmatically. The MRE itself notes the table is 'merely indicative' and recommends verifying directly with destination-country diplomatic missions. Destination data for Brazilian citizens is best derived by visa-matrix inversion from destination-country official sources.

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of Algerian diplomatic and official passports can enter Brazil without visas for a stay of up to 90 days (or up to the period of their mission, in case of Algerian officials accredited by the Brazilian government). For all other types of passport, a visa is required prior to entering Brazil.

For all other types of passport, a visa is required prior to entering Brazil.

cbi.notes: Brazil does not offer a citizenship-by-investment program. Brazil allows dual citizenship under its Constitution. Path to citizenship requires naturalization based on residence (minimum 4 years of uninterrupted residence for ordinary applicants). No direct citizenship-for-investment route exists. Source: Brazilian Ministry of Foreign Affairs official site (embassy Manila and Damascus pages on naturalization).

programs.name: Real Estate Investor Residence Permit (VIPER - Autorização de Residência como Investidor Imobiliário)
programs.notes: Minimum investment of R$1,000,000 in urban real estate in any Brazilian state (South/Southeast/Center-West), or R$700,000 in the North or Northeast regions of Brazil. Based on Normative Resolution RN 36/2018 (CNIg). Grants initial temporary residence of 4 years. Investment can be in one or multiple properties (aggregate value). After 4 years of residence, investor may apply for permanent residence and subsequently for Brazilian citizenship. Minimum physical presence: 14 days every 2 years to maintain status. Dual citizenship permitted under Brazilian constitution. Family members (spouse/partner and dependent children) included in same application. Processing: 6-18 months typically. Source confirmed from official MRE consulate pages (Doha, Amsterdam, Chicago) and portaldeimigracao.mj.gov.br

programs.name: Investment Visa - VITEM IX
programs.notes: Temporary investment visa for foreign nationals who wish to invest in Brazil (individuals, entrepreneurs, or company representatives). Standard minimum investment R$500,000 in a Brazilian company; reduced to R$150,000 for investments in priority sectors or regions designated by the National Immigration Council. Can lead to residence authorization. Real estate option: R$700,000 or R$1,000,000 depending on region per RN 36/2018. Applicants must first obtain prior residence authorization from Ministry of Justice via MigranteWeb portal.

programs.name: Digital Nomad Visa (VITEM XIV - Nômade Digital)
programs.eligibility: Foreign nationals who work remotely for foreign companies or clients outside Brazil, using information and communication technology. Must demonstrate minimum monthly income of USD 1,500 or available savings of at least USD 18,000. Valid passport, criminal background check (apostilled), health insurance with minimum USD 30,000 coverage required.
programs.processing_time: 15-30 business days (with assistance); 30-60 business days (self-filed); some consulates up to 3-6 months
programs.notes: Based on Normative Resolution RN 45/2021. Visa code VITEM XIV. Initial validity 1 year, extendable for 1 more year (maximum 2 years total residence under this category). Application fees: R$168.13 for in-country applications (MigranteWeb). Income must be from foreign-paying sources. Confirmed across multiple official MRE consulate pages (Helsinki, New York, London, Amsterdam, Houston, San Francisco, Zagreb). After meeting residency requirements, may transition to permanent residency and eventually naturalization.

programs.name: Retiree/Pensioner Residence Permit
programs.eligibility: Foreign retirees or pensioners receiving monthly income from foreign sources. Minimum income threshold required.
programs.notes: VITEM XIV category also covers retirees and pensioners. Official quick guide available from portaldeimigracao.mj.gov.br. Minimum income amount not confirmed verbatim from official source.

programs.name: Expedited Temporary Visa/Residence for Mercosur and CPLP Nationals
programs.eligibility: Nationals of Mercosur member/associate states and CPLP (Community of Portuguese-Speaking Countries) member states
programs.processing_time: Expedited under Portaria Interministerial MJSP/MRE nº 40/2023
programs.notes: Brazil expedited temporary visas and residence permits for Mercosur and Portuguese-language country migrants under Portaria Interministerial MJSP/MRE nº 40, of September 1, 2023 (CPLP) and a separate Portaria in October 2024 covering Mercosur nationals.

agreements: Mercosur (Mercado Comum do Sul) - freedom of movement and residence agreement for Argentina, Bolivia, Chile, Colombia, Ecuador, Paraguay, Peru, Uruguay; entry permitted with national ID
CPLP (Comunidade dos Países de Língua Portuguesa) Mobility Agreement - expedited temporary visas and residence for Portuguese-speaking country nationals
UNASUR (Union of South American Nations) - broader South American cooperation framework

validation_notes: OPEN DOORS 2026 (Portaria Interministerial 18/2026, effective 24 Feb 2026, published 4 Mar 2026): Eight countries granted new/modified visa-free access for ordinary passports - China (already listed, notes updated), Denmark (max_stay_days corrected from 90 to 30), France (max_stay_days corrected from 90 to 30; note added about temporary e-visa interlude Jan-Feb 2026 before restoration), Hungary (max_stay_days corrected from 90 to 30), Ireland (max_stay_days corrected from 90 to 30), Jamaica (max_stay_days corrected from 90 to 30), Saint Lucia (max_stay_days corrected from 90 to 30), Bahamas (max_stay_days corrected from 90 to 30). Under Portaria 18/2026 all eight get 30-day initial visa-free; extendable in-country to 90 days/12-month cumulative.

E-VISA EXPANSION 2026: Mexico added to e_visa list based on multiple consistent third-party reports (VisaHQ, TheTraveler) citing MRE December 2025 ordinance implementing permanent e-visa regime from January 1, 2026. However, official gov.br pages were CAPTCHA-blocked and could not be directly confirmed; source_official set to false. Mexico's classification is flagged as uncertain given its Mercosur associate status.

E-VISA FEE UPDATED: Prior record did not state the fee. Updated e_visa notes with the confirmed approximate fee of R$257 / USD 80.90 and multiple-entry validity up to 10 years (5 years for Canada/Australia) per consistent reporting.

CHINA note updated: Prior record cited a temporary exemption via MRE/MTur joint press release (30 days, May 11 - Dec 31, 2026). The Open Doors 2026 Portaria 18/2026 (effective Feb 24, 2026) also covers China under the same 30-day / extensible 90-day framework. Both are now reflected in China's notes.

VITEM IX min_amount field corrected from null to 500000 BRL: Standard business investment route requires R$500,000 minimum (reducible to R$150,000 for priority sectors per RN 36/2018), sourced from multiple official MRE consulate pages and third-party immigration lawyer sites. The prior record had min_amount as null.
VITEM IX notes expanded to clarify the R$500,000 standard and R$150,000 reduced-threshold option for priority sectors.

France temporarily placed under e-visa requirement (Jan 2026) then restored to visa-free under Open Doors 2026 (Feb 24, 2026) - this transition is documented in France's notes. Net current status: visa-free, 30 days/extendable.

2026-06-27 VALIDATION RUN: Re-fetched official gov.br sources - all major gov.br pages returned CAPTCHA blocks (0 official pages extractable). Verified via third-party and KPMG advisory. Key changes applied: (1) Mexico REMOVED from visa_free (was duplicated in both visa_free and e_visa); Mexico entry in e_visa retained and updated with consistent multi-source confirmation of e-visa requirement from Jan 1, 2026. (2) Argentina REMOVED from visa_free and ADDED to e_visa: multiple consistent third-party sources (VisaHQ Dec 2025, TheTraveler 2026, KPMG-cited Dec 2025 ordinance) confirm Argentina now requires e-visa from Jan 1, 2026 - source_official set to false as gov.br direct confirmation was blocked. (3) EU-Brazil Decree No. 12,864 (effective March 2, 2026) noted: formalizes 90/180 rolling window for EU Schengen ordinary-passport holders; does NOT cover UK or Ireland. (4) Open Doors 2026 pilot evaluation (Q4 2026) pending - the eight countries' visa-free status may change.

SOURCES REACHED (2026-06-27 run): gov.br = 0 (all CAPTCHA-blocked); kpmg.com = 1 (EU-Brazil Decree 12,864 confirmed); visahq.com/search = 3 (Open Doors 2026, e-visa expansion confirmed); wikipedia = 1 (partial, some data outdated); fragomen.com = 1 (humanitarian visa framework only). Total official pages loaded: 1 (KPMG). Third-party corroboration: multiple consistent sources for Argentina/Mexico e-visa.

2026-06-29 CORRECTION RUN: CRITICAL ERROR CORRECTED from 2026-06-27 run. (1) Argentina moved BACK to visa_free: The 2026-06-27 run incorrectly classified Argentina as e_visa based on speculative December 2025 VisaHQ articles predicting future policy changes. June 2026 verification via visabeat.com, joinsherpa.com, and web search synthesis confirms Argentina remains visa-free for entry to Brazil (90 days, national ID accepted at land borders). TheTraveler.org (Feb 6 2026) explicitly stated Argentina was NOT under e-visa at that date. No official gov.br ordinance adding Argentina to e-visa regime was found. (2) Mexico moved BACK to visa_free: The 2026-06-27 run incorrectly classified Mexico as e_visa. June 2026 verification via visarequirements.info and search synthesis confirms Mexico remains visa-free for entry to Brazil (90 days). Importantly, the articles about Mexico + e-visa were about Mexico requiring e-visa for Brazilians entering Mexico (reciprocal measure from Feb 5 2026), NOT Brazil requiring e-visa for Mexicans. Brazil has NOT yet formally reciprocated with an e-visa requirement for Mexican passport holders as of June 2026. (3) gov.br sources remain CAPTCHA-blocked. (4) SOURCES REACHED (2026-06-29 run): gov.br = 0 (CAPTCHA); US State Dept travel.state.gov = 1 (confirmed US e-visa, dated Aug 2025); KPMG = 1 (EU-Brazil decree confirmed); Wikipedia = 1 (partial); third-party aggregators (visabeat, joinsherpa, search) = multiple. Official pages loaded: 2 (US State Dept + KPMG). Classification safety: false visa-free is worst error; per safety rules, classified DOWN to visa-free for Argentina/Mexico since no confirmed official ordinance exists for e-visa requirement.
gaps: Official gov.br QGRV pages (January 15, 2026 English version at qgrv-simples-ing-15jan26.pdf) returned CAPTCHA block - unable to directly read the 2026 updated official table. Open Doors 2026 Portaria 18/2026 ordinance text itself was not directly fetched from gov.br/DOU (Diário Oficial da União) due to CAPTCHA restrictions; sourced from consistent third-party reporting (VisaHQ, StampedNomad, TravelAndTourWorld).

Mexico visa status (RESOLVED 2026-06-29): Mexico REMAINS visa-free for entry to Brazil as of June 2026. The 2026-06-27 run incorrectly classified Mexico as e-visa based on speculative articles about future plans. The e-visa narrative was about Mexico requiring e-visa for Brazilians (reverse direction, from Feb 5 2026), not Brazil requiring e-visa for Mexicans. Current classification: visa_free, 90 days. Monitor for any future Brazilian reciprocal measure against Mexican passports.

Argentina visa status (RESOLVED 2026-06-29): Argentina REMAINS visa-free for entry to Brazil as of June 2026. The 2026-06-27 run incorrectly classified Argentina as e-visa based on speculative VisaHQ Dec 2025 articles. Multiple June 2026 sources confirm Argentina remains visa-free under Mercosur arrangements. Current classification: visa_free, 90 days, national ID accepted.

Full list of nationalities required to obtain e-visa under the January 2026 permanent e-visa regime: confirmed only for USA, Canada, Australia (all effective April 10 2025 / permanent Jan 1 2026). France was briefly on e-visa then restored to visa-free Feb 24 2026. Gov.br pages remain CAPTCHA-blocked for definitive extraction of the full 30+ country list.

Open Doors 2026 pilot evaluation scheduled for Q4 2026 - the visa-free status of the eight new countries (Denmark, France, Hungary, Ireland, Jamaica, Bahamas, Saint Lucia) may revert to e-visa if the pilot is not extended.

China visa-free status: The record now reflects both the Open Doors 2026 framework (30 days, extendable) effective Feb 24, 2026, and the separate MRE/MTur bilateral press release (May 11 - Dec 31, 2026, 30 days for tourism/business). The interaction between these two instruments is not fully clear from available sources.

VIPER minimum investment amount: R$700,000 (North/Northeast) and R$1,000,000 (other regions) confirmed from multiple official MRE consulate pages citing RN 36/2018, but the specific portaldeimigracao.mj.gov.br PDF pages confirming updated 2026 amounts were not extractable.

Digital Nomad visa income requirement (USD 1,500/month or USD 18,000 savings): confirmed from multiple official MRE consulate pages but slight discrepancy (USD 1,500 vs USD 1,800) noted in some sources. The USD 1,500 figure is used here per majority of official consulate pages.
Kosovo status: Code 12 in QGRV - Brazil does not maintain diplomatic relations; visa may be granted for up to 90 days but not recorded as standard visa-free entry.
RASD (Western Sahara/Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic): Code 13 - Brazil does not maintain diplomatic relations; visa granted on laissez-passer. Not included in standard visa categories.

Conditional access Type A (credential-based entry based on holding a third-country visa such as US/Schengen/EU/UK/Canada visa, residence permit, Green Card/PR): No official Brazilian government source found for any such rule. Brazil's entry policy is nationality-based.
Conditional access Type B (OCI card or other diaspora/special status): No official Brazilian source found for any OCI cardholder or similar special status granting entry.
